topic.title: HTML Video Element

content.title: Captions Are a Legal and Ethical Requirement
content.text: WCAG 1.2.2 (Level AA) requires captions for all prerecorded synchronised video. Captions are not subtitles: captions describe all auditory content including speech, sound effects, and music cues for deaf and hard-of-hearing users. A <track kind='captions'> element with a valid WebVTT file satisfies this requirement. Omitting captions is the most common accessibility failure in video content and may constitute a legal violation under laws such as the ADA, the UK Equality Act, and India's RPwD Act.

examples.title: Video with Controls, Poster, Sources, and Captions Track
examples.description: This example shows a complete accessible video player: width and height to prevent layout shift, a poster image, a multi-format source chain (AV1, H.265, H.264), a captions track and a subtitles track, the playsinline attribute for iOS, and a fallback download link for unsupported browsers.
examples.code_snippet:
HTML Video Element

1. Complete accessible video with captions

2. WebVTT caption file format

Save as /tracks/lesson-01.en.vtt.

WEBVTT

00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:05.000
Welcome to the HTML Foundations course.

00:00:05.000 --> 00:00:12.500
In this first lesson, we will explore
the structure of an HTML document.

00:00:12.500 --> 00:00:18.000
[Keyboard typing sound]
Let's start by looking at the DOCTYPE declaration.

00:00:18.000 --> 00:00:24.000
[Background music fades out]
The DOCTYPE tells the browser which version
of HTML to expect.

Captions vs subtitles in WebVTT:
Captions include non-speech audio cues in square brackets: [Keyboard typing], [Music fades], [Applause].
Subtitles contain speech translation only.

3. Common mistakes

Wrong: no width/height — causes layout shift

<video controls src="lesson.mp4"></video>

Right: width and height reserve layout space

<video controls width="800" height="450" src="lesson.mp4"></video>

Wrong: autoplay without muted — blocked by browsers

<video autoplay src="intro.mp4"></video>

Right: autoplay requires muted

<video autoplay muted playsinline loop src="intro.mp4"></video>

Wrong: no captions track — WCAG 1.2.2 failure

<video controls src="lesson.mp4"></video>

Right: captions track attached

<video controls width="800" height="450">
  <source src="lesson.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <track kind="captions" src="lesson.en.vtt"
         srclang="en" label="English" default>
</video>
examples.output: Page renders three sections. The complete player shows a 16:9 video element with a poster placeholder, native controls, and the source chain annotated inline. Below the video a figcaption provides a transcript link. The WebVTT section shows a dark code block with a four-cue caption file including a sound effect cue in square brackets, followed by an annotation distinguishing captions from subtitles. The mistakes section shows three pairs of red and green code cards for missing dimensions, autoplay without muted, and missing captions track.

examples.title: Background Video, Custom Player, and Video Format Comparison
examples.description: This example shows a hero background video with autoplay muted playsinline loop, a custom accessible video player using the HTMLVideoElement JavaScript API with play/pause, seek, volume, fullscreen, and caption toggle controls, and a video format comparison table covering AV1, H.265, VP9, and H.264.
examples.code_snippet:
Video Patterns Reference

1. Background hero video (autoplay muted loop)

HTML Foundations Course

12 modules · 48 lessons · Certificate on completion

Background video: aria-hidden="true" hides the decorative video from screen readers. The text overlay is the meaningful content — it must not be conveyed only through the video.

2. Custom accessible video player

0:00
0:00

3. Video format browser support

CodecContainertype value ChromeFirefoxSafariEdge Quality
AV1MP4 / WebM video/mp4; codecs=av01... 70+67+ 17+79+ Best
H.265 (HEVC)MP4 video/mp4; codecs=hvc1 NoNo YesHW only Excellent (Apple)
VP9WebM video/webm; codecs=vp9 YesYes 14.1+Yes Very good
H.264 (AVC)MP4 video/mp4 YesYes YesYes Good (universal)
VP8WebM video/webm; codecs=vp8 YesYes NoYes Acceptable

Recommended source order: AV1 → H.265 (for Apple) → H.264 (universal fallback)
examples.output: Page renders three sections. The background hero shows a 260px high section with a video element absolutely positioned as a dark overlay, text content centred on top, and a note about aria-hidden on decorative video. The custom player shows a dark card with a video element, a controls bar with play, progress, time readouts, a CC button, and a fullscreen button, all wired by JavaScript including the TextTrack API for caption toggling. The format table shows five codecs with browser columns colour-coded green, amber, and red, with a recommended source order note below.

because no single codec is supported by all browsers. AV1 offers the best compression ratio but requires hardware decoding support for smooth high-resolution playback. H.264 has the broadest support but larger file sizes. H.265 excels on Apple hardware. The
attributes lets each browser silently select its optimal codec at zero extra network cost.

subtopics.title: Video Formats and Source
subtopics.description: source children with type give the browser a codec fallback from best compression to widest support.

text.value: attribute for client-side codec negotiation. The four codecs in common web use are
(universal fallback). AV1 in an MP4 container is the modern first choice. H.264 in MP4 remains the universal last fallback. The
attribute specifies the exact codec profile, enabling the browser to confirm support without a network request. Reference:

examples.title: Source Chain, MIME Types, and canPlayType Detection
examples.description: This example shows the recommended three-source fallback chain for web video, the exact type attribute strings for every codec, a live JavaScript canPlayType detector that shows which codecs the current browser supports, and wrong versus right source ordering patterns.
examples.code_snippet:
Video Source Formats

1. Recommended three-source fallback chain

<video controls width="800" height="450"
       poster="/img/poster.jpg" preload="metadata">

  <!-- 1st: AV1 — best compression, all modern browsers -->
  <source src="/video/lesson.av1.mp4"
          type="video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08">

  <!-- 2nd: H.265 — Apple devices (Safari, iOS) -->
  <source src="/video/lesson.hevc.mp4"
          type="video/mp4; codecs=hvc1">

  <!-- 3rd: H.264 — universal fallback -->
  <source src="/video/lesson.h264.mp4"
          type="video/mp4">

  <!-- Text track for accessibility -->
  <track kind="captions" src="/tracks/lesson.en.vtt"
         srclang="en" label="English" default>

  <!-- Download fallback -->
  <p>Download: <a href="/video/lesson.h264.mp4">lesson.mp4</a></p>

</video>

2. type attribute strings by codec

Codectype valueNotes
AV1 (MP4) video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08 Profile 0, level 5, 8-bit
AV1 (WebM) video/webm; codecs=av01.0.05M.08 WebM container variant
H.265 / HEVC video/mp4; codecs=hvc1 Safari and iOS only
VP9 (WebM) video/webm; codecs=vp9 Chrome, Firefox, Edge
H.264 / AVC video/mp4 Universal; no codec param needed
VP8 (WebM) video/webm; codecs=vp8 Legacy; avoid in new projects

3. canPlayType live detection

Video codec support in this browser:

    4. Source order mistakes

    Wrong: H.264 first — modern browsers never try AV1

    <source src="lesson.h264.mp4" type="video/mp4">
<source src="lesson.av1.mp4"  type="video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08">

    Right: best codec first, universal fallback last

    <source src="lesson.av1.mp4"  type="video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08">
<source src="lesson.h264.mp4" type="video/mp4">

    Wrong: no type attribute — forces speculative fetches

    <source src="/video/lesson.av1.mp4">

    Right: type attribute enables zero-cost negotiation

    <source src="/video/lesson.av1.mp4" type="video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08">
examples.output: Page renders four sections. The source chain section shows a dark code block with a three-source fallback, a track element, and a download fallback paragraph. The MIME type table lists six codecs with exact type strings and notes. The canPlayType section shows a live JavaScript detector that lists each codec with a colour-coded support result. The mistakes section shows four code cards alternating wrong and right for source order and missing type attribute.

text.value: tier), 8-bit colour depth. Profile 0 is the widest-supported AV1 profile. Writing this full
string allows browsers to confirm hardware-accelerated AV1 support before fetching the file, preventing software-decoding fallback on devices that cannot decode AV1 in hardware.
Unlike audio, video codec selection is also constrained by
but fall back to software decoding on older devices, causing high CPU usage and dropped frames. The

for a specific codec, resolution, and bitrate combination.
navigator.mediaCapabilities.decodingInfo({ type: 'file', video: { contentType: 'video/mp4; codecs=av01.0.05M.08', width: 1920, height: 1080, bitrate: 3000000, framerate: 30 } })
are true, otherwise fall back to H.264.

subtopics.title: Video Captions and Tracks
subtopics.description: The track element attaches WebVTT captions, subtitles, and chapters to satisfy WCAG 1.2.2.

text.value: text track file to its parent
attribute provides the human-readable name shown in the captions menu. The
boolean attribute activates the track on load; only one
must describe all auditory content, not just speech. Reference:

content.title: WebVTT File Must Be Served from the Same Origin or with CORS
content.text: Browsers enforce the same-origin policy on WebVTT track files loaded by <track>. A VTT file hosted on a different domain will be blocked unless the server sends Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. This is the most common reason captions appear to load but are never displayed. When using a CDN for VTT files, configure CORS headers on the CDN origin.

examples.title: WebVTT Format, Multiple Tracks, and TextTrack API
examples.description: This example shows the complete WebVTT file format with cue timing, speaker labels, and sound effect annotations, a video element with four track elements covering captions, Hindi subtitles, audio descriptions, and chapters, and the JavaScript TextTrack API for programmatic caption control including toggle, language switching, and cuechange events.
examples.code_snippet:
WebVTT Captions and Tracks

1. WebVTT file format

WEBVTT

<!-- File must begin with "WEBVTT" on the first line -->

<!-- Basic cue: timestamp --> timestamp, then caption text -->
00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:04.500
Welcome to the HTML Foundations course.

<!-- Multi-line cue -->
00:00:04.500 --> 00:00:10.000
In this lesson we will cover the
structure of an HTML document.

<!-- Speaker label (by convention, not a VTT standard) -->
00:00:10.000 --> 00:00:16.000
<v Priya>The DOCTYPE declaration tells
the browser which HTML version to use.

<!-- Sound effect — REQUIRED for WCAG 1.2.2 captions -->
00:00:16.000 --> 00:00:19.000
[Keyboard typing]

<!-- Music cue -->
00:00:19.000 --> 00:00:22.500
[Upbeat intro music plays]

<!-- Named cue identifier (optional) -->
section-intro
00:00:22.500 --> 00:00:28.000
Let's open a code editor and write
our first HTML document together.

2. Video with captions, subtitles, descriptions, chapters

3. TextTrack API: toggle and language switch

Current cue text:

No cue active

4. track kind attribute reference

kindTarget userContentWCAG
captions Deaf / hard-of-hearing All audio: speech + sound effects + music 1.2.2 (AA)
subtitles Non-native speakers Speech translation only 1.2.2 (partially)
descriptions Blind / low vision Visual content not in audio 1.2.3 / 1.2.5 (AA)
chapters All users Named navigation points Navigation aid
metadata JavaScript only Timed data for scripts; not displayed N/A
examples.output: Page renders four sections. The WebVTT format section shows a dark code block with six cue examples covering basic cues, multi-line, speaker labels, sound effects, music cues, and named identifiers. The four-track video shows a player with all four track types declared. The TextTrack API section shows three toggle buttons (Off, English, Hindi) and a live cue display area wired to the cuechange event with an aria-live region. The kind reference table lists all five track kinds with target user, content description, and WCAG criterion.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Video Element | video, source, track, captions Complete Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Master the HTML video element: source codec fallbacks, WebVTT captions with track element, poster and playsinline attributes, custom JavaScript players, and WCAG 1.2.2 compliance.
